name: Java String immutability
question_text:
In Java, consider the following snippet:

String s = "hi";
s.concat(" there");
System.out.println(s);

What is printed to the console?
general_feedback: String objects are immutable in Java — every method that appears to modify a string actually returns a new string. s.concat(" there") produces a new String with value "hi there", but the reference s is never reassigned, so it still points at the original "hi".
correct_feedback: Correct — strings are immutable, so the concat result is discarded.
incorrect_feedback: Incorrect — think about what concat returns, and whether s itself is reassigned.

answers.feedback: Right — concat returns a new String, and s was never reassigned to it.
That would require reassigning s, e.g. s = s.concat(" there"). The snippet discards the returned String.
The snippet is valid Java. String has a concat method that returns String.
s is initialised to a non-null literal, so no NullPointerException is thrown.
